figures.caption: Figure 13: Comparison of exceedance probability estimates. Panel (a) shows the Jensen–Shannon distance between the two models, and panel (b) shows the log-odds difference indicating which model predicts higher exceedance probabilities.

Figure 14: Comparison of the predicted PM10 densities for three municipalities (Milano, Ravenna and Imperia). The blue curve corresponds to the coFRK model, while the grey curve corresponds to the SDE approach.
Figure 9: Spatial distribution of the average PM10 concentration per monitoring station across Northern Italy (2018–2022).

Figure 1: Left: spatial sampling design used in the simulation study, with training locations shown as circles and test locations as crosses. Right: BAU grid and bisquare basis functions used in the two-resolution system, showing basis centers and supports at the coarse and fine levels.
Figure 6: RMSE (left) and R2 (right) for Z1 as a function of the missing-area proportion.

Figure 7: Comparison between the true field Z1(s) and the predicted fields from FRK and coFRK. The red border in the True field panel indicates the region where Z1(s) is treated as unobserved in the experiment. Predictions are shown at the BAU level.
Figure 10: Spatial distribution of (a) the mean PM10 concentration and (b) the 95th percentile (Q95) estimated from the reconstructed density functions.

Table 6: Summary of all functions implemented for the multivariate FRK model. Only the high-level functions in Section (A) are intended to be called by the user; sections (B)–(D) are internal routines automatically executed within SRE mv.fit() and SRE mv.predict()
Figure 8: Improvement in RMSE of co-kriging with respect to FRK as a function of the proportion of unobserved area.
Figure 5: RMSE of coFRK and FRK as a function of the inter-variable correlation parameter r0. Vertical bars indicate ±1 standard deviation across Monte Carlo replications.
Figure 16: Comparison between the true field Z1(s) and the predicted fields from FRK and coFRK. Predictions are shown at the BAU level.

Figure 15: Parameter estimates of σ2 s , σ2 ξ , and κ0 across the 50 Monte Carlo replications. The boxplots summarize the empirical variability of the estimates across simulations, while the red dashed lines indicate the true parameter values used in the data-generating process.
Table 4: Estimated spatial variance σ̂2 s for different values of the true fine-scale variance σ2 ξ , before (left panel) and after (right panel) applying ridge penalization.
Figure 2: Functional boxplots for the three spatial correlation scenarios.
